name: Great Zimbabwe
description: Great Zimbabwe is an ancient city in the south-eastern hills of Zimbabwe. Construction began in the 11th century and continued until it was abandoned in the 15th century. The towers were erected by ancestors of the Shona people currently found in Zimbabwe and nearby countries. It spans an area of 1,780 acres (7.2 square kilometers) and could have housed up to 18,000 people. It is recognized as a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The name 'Zimbabwe' itself comes from the Shona term 'dzimba dza mabwe', translated as 'houses of stone'. Some alternative theories, often rooted in colonial-era racist beliefs, posited that Great Zimbabwe was built by non-African civilizations, such as the Phoenicians, Egyptians, or even the Queen of Sheba. These theories were fueled by a desire to deny African agency and sophistication. Proponents of these theories often point to the impressive scale of the structures and the perceived lack of evidence for advanced construction techniques among indigenous populations. Mainstream archaeological and historical research, however, overwhelmingly supports the indigenous Shona origin of Great Zimbabwe. Archaeological evidence, including pottery, iron tools, and oral traditions, firmly links the site to the Shona people and their ancestors. Radiocarbon dating confirms the construction timeline, and comparative analysis with other Shona settlements reveals similar architectural styles and cultural practices. The site is now a symbol of Zimbabwean national identity and pride, representing the rich history and cultural heritage of the Shona people.
subtitle: Ancient city of the Shona people.

claims.claim: Great Zimbabwe was built by non-African civilizations (e.g., Phoenicians, Egyptians).
claims.evidence: Scale of the structures, perceived lack of advanced technology among indigenous populations, some superficial similarities to Middle Eastern architecture.
claims.criticism: Overwhelming archaeological evidence (pottery, tools, radiocarbon dating) points to Shona origin. Similar structures found in other Shona settlements. Racist origins of the claim aimed at denying African agency.

claims.claim: Great Zimbabwe was the capital of the Queen of Sheba.
claims.evidence: Biblical references to gold and wealth, perceived similarities between the name 'Zimbabwe' and place names in the Middle East.
claims.criticism: No archaeological or historical evidence to support this claim. Geographic and chronological inconsistencies. Based on speculation and romanticized interpretations of the Bible.

claims.claim: The builders possessed advanced, lost knowledge or technology.
claims.evidence: Precision of stone cutting and construction, alignment with astronomical events (sometimes unsubstantiated).
claims.criticism: Stone cutting techniques were sophisticated but not beyond the capabilities of skilled artisans. Alignments are debated and often exaggerated. No evidence of lost technology beyond known methods.

keyFigures.name: Karl Mauch
keyFigures.role: German explorer who initially attributed the site to foreign builders.

keyFigures.name: David Randall-MacIver
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist who first scientifically attributed the site to indigenous Africans.

keyFigures.name: Gertrude Caton-Thompson
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist whose excavations confirmed the Shona origin of the site.

mythologyConnections: Mwari (Shona supreme being) - connection to spiritual significance of the site
Oral traditions of the Shona people relating to kings and ancestral spirits associated with the site.

timeline.event: Peak of Great Zimbabwe's power and influence.
European explorers 'rediscover' the site and propose non-African origins.
Scientific archaeological investigations confirm Shona origins.
Great Zimbabwe is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and a symbol of Zimbabwean national identity.

sources.author: Hall, Martin
sources.work: Farmers, Kings, and Traders: The People of Southern Africa, 200-1860
sources.title: Hall, Martin
